skill.name: code-reviewer
skill.description: Structured code review skill with Python helper scripts and reference guides for reviewing pull requests, checking code quality, and generating review reports.
skill.summary: Review code changes with structured checklists, local helper scripts, and report guidance.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: AI review found no prompt injection, network access, subprocess execution, or weak cryptography despite the static high-risk score. The confirmed risk is local filesystem writes to user-supplied output paths in helper scripts, while command, .env, and weak-crypto matches are documentation or string false positives. Overall risk is medium because running community scripts still gives local file write capability.

medium_findings.title: Caller-Controlled File Writes in Helper Scripts
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The three Python helper scripts write JSON output to the path supplied through --output. This is legitimate report behavior, but it can overwrite files accessible to the process if run with a sensitive path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and present in all three scripts. The risk is bounded because the destination is user supplied and there is no evidence of hidden writes or network exfiltration.

low_findings.title: External Command Matches Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static command detections occur in Markdown usage blocks and reference code fences. I did not find subprocess, os.system, shell execution, or Ruby backtick execution in the executable Python scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported locations are Markdown code blocks or prose, not executable skill logic. A broad search found no runtime command execution APIs in the scripts.

low_findings.title: Environment File Mention Is Setup Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: SKILL.md shows cp .env.example .env as a setup command. No script reads .env files, environment variables, secrets, tokens, or credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious text is a visible setup example in documentation. I found no corresponding code path that opens environment files or extracts secret values.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported weak-cryptography locations are prose, headings, or parser description strings. I did not find MD5, SHA1, encryption, hashing, or cryptographic API usage in the scanned files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact reported lines do not contain cryptographic operations. Searches for common crypto APIs and weak hash names did not reveal supporting evidence.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Matches Are Generic Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported reconnaissance locations are generic checklist phrases such as anti-patterns and code examples. No evidence found of host enumeration, system profiling, or data collection commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are section labels or documentation bullets. I found no commands or APIs that collect operating system, network, user, or process information.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unconstrained Output Path Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper scripts open the --output path directly for writing. This is useful for reports, but users should avoid running the scripts with elevated privileges or writing into sensitive locations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and repeated in each script. It is a moderate local file safety concern, not evidence of malicious intent.

content.user_title: Review Code Changes with Structured Feedback
content.value_statement: Code reviews can miss consistency, security, and maintainability issues when teams rely on ad hoc comments. This skill provides checklists, local helper scripts, and report guidance for repeatable review workflows.
actual_capabilities: Provides Markdown reference guides for review checklists, coding standards, and common anti-patterns.
Includes Python command-line scripts for PR analysis, code quality checks, and review report generation.
Accepts a target path and validates that the path exists before producing a simple report.
Can print verbose progress messages for local review runs.
Can emit JSON-formatted results to standard output or a selected output file.
Documents common development, testing, build, and deployment commands for reviewer context.
limitations: The included scripts are scaffolds and currently return empty findings unless extended.
The skill does not integrate with GitHub, GitLab, or CI systems by itself.
The scripts can write to a caller-selected output path, so users should choose safe destinations.
Reference documents are generic and may need team-specific review rules.

use_cases.title: Standardize Pull Request Reviews
use_cases.description: Use the checklists to keep feedback consistent across maintainability, testing, security, and performance topics.

use_cases.title: Create Review Reports Locally
use_cases.description: Run the helper scripts against a repository path and save simple JSON output for review notes.

use_cases.title: Teach Review Practices
use_cases.description: Use the reference files as starter material for code review onboarding and quality discussions.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Small Change
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a focused diff
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code change for correctness, readability, and missing tests. Give concise findings with file and line references when possible.

prompt_templates.title: Check Pull Request Quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the code-reviewer checklist to assess this pull request. Separate blocking issues, suggestions, and questions for the author.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Security and Maintainability
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this module for input validation, authorization, error handling, dependency risk, and long-term maintainability. Prioritize concrete defects over style comments.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Release Review Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these changes as a release gate. Summarize residual risk, test gaps, migration concerns, and the minimum fixes needed before merge.

output_examples.input: A pull request modifies an API handler and adds a database query.
output: Blocking: The handler does not verify the caller can access the requested account.
Suggestion: Add an integration test for the empty result case.
Question: Should this query include pagination for large accounts?

output_examples.input: A developer asks for a maintainability review of a service module.
output: The module has clear boundaries, but error handling is inconsistent across public methods.
Extracting shared validation would reduce repeated checks and simplify future changes.
Add tests for timeout handling before relying on this in production.

output_examples.input: A team wants a review summary before merging a release branch.
output: Merge risk is moderate because database changes lack rollback notes.
Test coverage covers core flows but misses permission failures.
Recommended next step: add rollback documentation and one authorization regression test.

best_practices: Run helper scripts with normal user privileges and write output to a project-local report path.
Use the checklist as a starting point, then add repository-specific standards and risk areas.
Ask for findings ordered by severity so review comments focus on merge-blocking issues first.
anti_patterns: Do not treat generated feedback as a substitute for tests, local builds, or domain review.
Do not run community scripts against sensitive directories without inspecting the script behavior first.
Do not request broad style rewrites when the review goal is to find defects before merge.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically review GitHub pull requests?
faq.answer: No. It provides local scripts and prompts, but it does not connect to GitHub or other code hosting services.

faq.question: Can it find real code defects?
faq.answer: It can guide an AI-assisted review, but the included scripts are scaffolds and currently return empty findings.

faq.question: Is it safe to run the scripts?
faq.answer: The scripts are local and show no network behavior, but they can write to a user-selected output path.

faq.question: Which languages does it target?
faq.answer: The documentation mentions TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Swift, Kotlin, and Go review workflows.

faq.question: Does it replace human code review?
faq.answer: No. It helps structure review work and surface issues, but humans should make merge decisions.

faq.question: How should teams customize it?
faq.answer: Teams should add project-specific standards, security requirements, and test expectations to the reference guides.
